Its category is: Generator failures, grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Summer 2019-20 to Summer 2023-24. Its root cause is: Aging coal fleets, generator failures, and increased demand during summer peaks. Its accident scale is: Potentially affecting up to 1.3 million Victorian homes. Its dates are: 22-08-2019. Its affected region is: Victoria and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Residential users in Victoria and potentially New South Wales. Its cause type is: Infrastructure and demand-related. Its causes are: Aging coal fleet, generator failures, increased demand during summer peaks.

title: The Fuel Mix supporting the NEM is at the start of a massive transformation
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Fuel Mix supporting the NEM is at the start of a massive transformation," posted at 20-08-2019 02:01:00 PM is: The news article discusses an analytical report highlighting the transformation of the fuel mix in the National Electricity Market (NEM). As of 2018, coal-fired generation supplied around 75% of the grid's energy, although its capacity is declining. This transformation involves a significant increase in renewable energy sources, such as wind and solar, despite the current dominance of coal. The report underlines the increasing risks to the electricity market as the transition progresses and coal plants close. The article emphasizes the complexity and importance of managing these closures properly, referencing prior closures such as Hazelwood, which showed examples of mishandling the transition. The transformation, though significant, is still in its early stages. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event's impact on the electricity market is: Medium to long term. Its root cause is: The shift from coal to renewable energy sources in the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-08-2019. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy sector stakeholders in the NEM. Its cause type is: Structural. Its causes are: Decline in coal-fired power capacity, increase in renewables, risks associated with coal plant closures.

title: Transitional challenges require greatly enhanced analytical capability (exemplified in MT PASA Rule Change proposals, for instance)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Transitional challenges require greatly enhanced analytical capability (exemplified in MT PASA Rule Change proposals, for instance)" posted at 15-08-2019 12:29:00 PM is: On 14th August 2019, discussions were conducted in Sydney with the Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) regarding two rule change proposals by ERM Power aimed at improving the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) MT PASA process. The proposals include enhancing data transparency, extending the methodology from deterministic to probabilistic processes, and increasing the time range covered by MT PASA from two to three years to align with the Retailer Reliability Obligation. The proposed changes are intended to boost analytical capability to deal with transitional challenges in the energy sector. The AEMC is considering these changes to support wholesale and retail market participants. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Uncertain. Its root_cause is: Needs for improved transparency and methodology in energy management processes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14th August 2019 (meeting conducted), 21st August 2019 (next event planned). Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO), wholesale and retail electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Policy initiative. Its causes are: Calls for more transparent data, methodical insights for energy transition, and enhanced analytical capabilities.

title: 5 FAQs we’re asked about the Generator Report Card (for discussion at AIE this evening)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "5 FAQs we’re asked about the Generator Report Card (for discussion at AIE this evening)" posted at 14-08-2019 11:52:00 AM is: The news article discusses the presentation given by Jonathon Dyson of Greenview Strategic Consulting and another co-author regarding the Generator Report Card at an event organized by the Australian Institute of Energy. The Generator Report Card, released on 31st May 2019, encompasses a detailed analysis of the last 20 years of generation history in the National Electricity Market (NEM) and anticipates future trends. It addresses reasons for the report's development, including the 20-year milestone of the NEM, confusion and misunderstandings in the industry, the need for intensive R&D, and natural curiosity. The report contains significant analysis, with its audience spanning government departments, market bodies, direct NEM participants, and various stakeholders. The challenges of data fit-for-purpose from AEMO’s MMS database in preparing the report are noted. It also provides guidance on accessing the Generator Report Card. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Comprehensive analysis for educational and strategic purposes. Its accident scale is: None. Its date is: 14-08-2019. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Government departments, market bodies, NEM participants, and other stakeholders. Its cause type is: Analysis and reporting. Its causes are: 20-year milestone of NEM, confusion and misunderstanding in the industry, R&D needs, curiosity, data analysis challenges.

title: Initial thoughts and questions about last Friday’s under-frequency load shedding in Great Britain
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Initial thoughts and questions about last Friday’s under-frequency load shedding in Great Britain" posted at 12-08-2019 02:17:00 PM is: On August 9, 2019, Great Britain experienced an under-frequency load shedding event as automatic systems were triggered due to a rapid drop in electricity generation. The sequence of events included the Little Barford CCGT plant and Hornsea Wind Farm going offline, reportedly due to a lightning strike, causing a loss of approximately 1,900 MW of power. This led to a frequency drop below 49 Hz that activated load shedding protocols. The National Grid and regulatory body Ofgem initiated investigations into the event to understand its causes and implications fully. The public and media showed considerable interest in identifying the contributing factors to prevent future occurrences; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: Sudden generation loss due to a lightning strike. Its accident scale is: Significant, impacting widespread areas in England and Wales. Its dates are: 09-08-2019. Its affected region is: Great Britain, particularly England and Wales. Its affected key users are: General public and industries depending on continuous power supply. Its cause type is: External natural event (lightning strike). Its causes are: Lightning strike, loss of generation at Little Barford CCGT, loss of generation at Hornsea Wind Farm, automated load shedding due to frequency drop.

title: Calculated level of inertia in the South Australian grid, supplied by synchronous generation plant
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2016 - 28th September - State-Wide Blackout in South Australia
classified_content: The summary of the news "Calculated level of inertia in the South Australian grid, supplied by synchronous generation plant" posted at 12-08-2019 07:54:00 AM is: The article discusses a recent significant automated under-frequency load shedding event in Great Britain that prompted discussions about system security in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly focusing on the level of inertia provided by synchronous generation in South Australia. The Generator Report Card analyzed how instantaneous levels of inertia in the SA grid were calculated, especially during the state-wide blackout on September 28, 2016, stressing the importance of system frequency and contrasting it with similar challenges faced in the UK. The discussions aim to explore contributing factors to grid instability as well as to encourage dialogue around system security and the insights from the Generator Report Card.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: System frequency issues in the grid. Its accident scale is: State-wide. Its dates are: 12-08-2019. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: System instability. Its causes are: Automated under-frequency load shedding event, system frequency challenges, synchronous generation inertia evaluation.

title: Heightened Risk an increasing feature in the NEM these days…
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Heightened Risk an increasing feature in the NEM these days…" posted at 09-08-2019 02:15:00 PM is: The article discusses the increasing financial risks in the National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly highlighted by extreme price fluctuations, such as prices hitting the Market Price Floor of –$1,000/MWh in South Australia. These fluctuations illustrate the market's volatility and the challenges in maintaining stability. Historical data shows multiple instances of daily average prices falling below $0/MWh, indicating a trend of financial instability. The article references the Generator Report Card, which delves into these risks and their implications for system security. The content underscores the need for ongoing analysis and adaptation within the energy market to manage these risks effectively. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, with ongoing potential for recurrence. Its root_cause is: Market volatility and pricing mechanisms within the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-08-2019, with historical data mentioning instances before and after this date. Its affected region is: South Australia, with mentions of other regions like QLD, VIC, and TAS. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants. Its cause type is: Financial/market instability. Its causes are: Extreme price volatility in the electricity market, historical instances of negative pricing, and market design factors.

title: Summer 19-20 is heating up already, with Reserve Trader called
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Summer 19-20 is heating up already, with Reserve Trader called" posted at 09-08-2019 10:43:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has initiated the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) Panel for all five regions in anticipation of the summer 2019-20 across the National Electricity Market (NEM), with memories of past grid issues and concerns over Loy Yang A2 and Mortlake 2 generators. This move is set against the backdrop of a potential permanent rule change for the use of Negawatts. Interested parties have until August 29, 2019, to express their interest, which reflects urgency in preparation ahead of the summer season starting in three months.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Summer 2019-20. Its root_cause is: Concerns over generator reliability and anticipated grid instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 09-08-2019 10:43:00 AM. Its affected region is: All five regions of the National Electricity Market in Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Preventative action. Its causes is: Past grid issues, concerns about generator reliability, potential for tight supply/demand balance.

title: Two other opportunities to meet with us in Sydney during August, and discuss the Generator Report Card
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two other opportunities to meet with us in Sydney during August, and discuss the Generator Report Card" posted at 09-08-2019 10:07:00 AM is: The article announces opportunities for stakeholders to discuss the Generator Report Card, developed with Greenview Strategic Consulting, at events in Sydney organized by the Australian Institute of Energy and the Australian Energy Council. These gatherings, scheduled for August 14th and August 21st, aim to share insights from the report and gather feedback from attendees involved in the Australian energy sector.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: August 14, 2019, and August 21, 2019. Its affected region is: Sydney, Australia. Its affected key users are: Industry stakeholders and potential report purchasers. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes are: Engagement with stakeholders and discussions on the Generator Report Card.

title: Two different storm warnings in South Australia – one literal, and one metaphorical
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2016 - 28th September - State-Wide Blackout in South Australia
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two different storm warnings in South Australia – one literal, and one metaphorical" posted at 08-08-2019 10:08:00 PM is: On August 8, 2019, South Australia faced both literal and metaphorical storm warnings affecting the electricity market. The literal storm involved severe weather with destructive winds prompting the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) to declare a "Protected Event" due to risks of multiple transmission elements' loss causing generation disconnection, leading to constraints on power flow between Victoria and South Australia to ensure system stability. Concurrently, a metaphorical storm brewed as the Australian Energy Regulator (AER) commenced court proceedings against four wind farm operators for alleged breaches of the National Electricity Rules following the 2016 South Australia state-wide blackout, raising concerns over generator performance standards and energy transition debates. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate for severe weather impacts; medium to long term for legal and regulatory outcomes. Its root_cause is: Severe weather conditions and legal/regulatory challenges from past events. Its accident scale is: Regional impact due to potential generation disconnection. Its dates are: 08-08-2019. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural and legal/regulatory issues. Its causes are: Severe weather with high winds, legal action against wind farm operators for national electricity rule breaches.

title: Questions and Observations–about AGL’s Liddell, Torrens A (and Loy Yang A) stations
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Questions and Observations–about AGL’s Liddell, Torrens A (and Loy Yang A) stations" posted at 07-08-2019 07:56:00 PM is: AGL Energy has decided to extend the operation of two of its power stations, Liddell (coal-fired) in New South Wales and Torrens A (gas-fired) in South Australia, beyond their scheduled closure dates to ensure sufficient dispatchable capacity during peak periods. This extension, intended to cover the high-demand summer seasons of 2019-20 and 2022-23, comes amidst various operational challenges such as significant outages at Loy Yang A2 and Mortlake unit 2. The extensions have triggered discussions about the reliability of aging coal plants and the adequacy of replacement capacity. The situation has been met with skepticism, particularly in light of the broader energy transition and questions about broader implications for energy reliability and market stability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Summer 2019-20 and Summer 2022-23. Its root_cause is: Operational extensions of aging power plants due to capacity concerns. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-08-2019, with reference to events on 02-08-2019. Its affected region is: New South Wales and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the regions relying on Liddell and Torrens A for electricity supply. Its cause type is: Decisions on energy infrastructure operation. Its causes are: Extension of Liddell and Torrens A operation, outages at Loy Yang A2, and potential capacity shortfalls.

title: Assessing the diversity of intermittent (wind and solar output) as one piece of analysis in the Generator Report Card
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Assessing the diversity of intermittent (wind and solar output) as one piece of analysis in the Generator Report Card" posted at 26-07-2019 05:29:00 PM is: The article discusses an analysis on the diversity of intermittent renewable energy sources, specifically wind and solar, using NEMreview software. The analysis compares outputs over nine months, including data from new and existing wind farms in Queensland. Three main points are highlighted: understanding outcome types (high correlation, randomness, high anti-correlation), correlation degrees between renewable plants, and hypothetical wind production in Renewable Energy Zones. The report emphasizes the importance of these insights for effective energy transition rather than relying on presumed randomness or diversity as a solution. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Analysis of renewable energy output diversity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-07-2019 05:29:00 PM. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Analytical study. Its causes are: Diversity analysis of wind and solar outputs, correlation studies of Renewable Energy Zones, hypothetical modeling of wind production.

title: War of Losses
author: Derek Chapman
classified_content: The summary of the news "War of Losses" posted at 26-07-2019 08:41:00 AM is: The article discusses the inefficiencies in the current Marginal Loss Factors (MLF) framework used in the National Electricity Market (NEM)'s settlement process. The author argues that the existing rules lead to significant inaccuracies, thus distorting the market by affecting operational and investment decisions. To address these issues, several proposals for rule enhancements are suggested, including sharing the "Intra-Regional Settlements Residue" (IRSR) with generators and using average or compressed loss factors instead of marginal ones. These changes aim to reduce inaccuracies and improve fairness, particularly as the IRSR highlights the growing error in the current MLF process. The article emphasizes the need for a more accurate calculation of losses that consider the complex AC transmission system, advocating for a revision of the MLF framework consistent with present-day realities. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Outdated MLF framework causing market inefficiencies. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-07-2019. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), primarily Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Generators and other market participants. Its cause type is: Market regulation inefficiency. Its causes are: Inaccurate MLF rules, fraudulent calculation assumptions, outdated economic constructs.

title: Keeping the lights on this summer – possible impact of the Loy Yang A2 and Mortlake outages
author: Allan O'Neil
topic: Seasonal Record of Events in the Market
classified_content: The summary of the news "Keeping the lights on this summer – possible impact of the Loy Yang A2 and Mortlake outages" posted at 23-07-2019 03:02:00 PM is: Two major thermal generating units in Victoria, Loy Yang A unit 2 and Mortlake unit 2, with a combined capacity of over 800 MW, have experienced significant electrical faults that necessitate extensive repairs, expected to be completed by December. These outages represent about 7% of Victoria's large-scale generating capacity and nearly 9% of its dispatchable capacity, raising valid concerns about supply reliability during the tight supply-demand balance anticipated over the 2019-20 summer. The outages have already influenced forward electricity market prices, particularly for Q1 2020, which anticipates further reliability challenges as both units may not return to service on time. Forward market contracts and AEMO assessments indicate that the situation could mirror the tight supply-demand conditions experienced in January 2019, with risks of load shedding remaining significant if repairs are delayed. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Breakdowns & Failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Mid-November 2019 to February 2020. Its root_cause is: Electrical faults in the thermal generating units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-07-2019 to expected repair completion in December 2019. Its affected region is: Victoria. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in Victoria. Its cause type is: Technical/Mechanical Failure. Its causes are: Electrical faults in Loy Yang A2 and Mortlake 2 requiring extensive repairs.

title: Spot power at $0/MWh in all regions (Sign ‘o the Times*?)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot power at $0/MWh in all regions (Sign ‘o the Times*?)" posted at 22-07-2019 10:16:00 AM is: Unusual market conditions were observed in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on Sunday 21st July 2019, where dispatch prices dropped to $0/MWh across the mainland regions, and –$0.01/MWh in Tasmania. This occurrence reflects the complexities and challenges associated with the energy market transition, emphasizing the need for storage solutions. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term influence on market perceptions and possible long-term implications for energy transitions. Its root_cause is: Unusual market conditions leading to negative or zero dispatch prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-07-2019. Its affected region is: All mainland regions in the NEM, and Tasmania. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants, particularly those without integrated storage solutions. Its cause type is: Market dynamics and unusual price outcomes. Its causes are: $0/MWh dispatch prices across the mainland; –$0.01/MWh in Tasmania; broader implications of the energy transition.

title: A quick look at Mortlake Unit 2 outage
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at Mortlake Unit 2 outage" posted at 09-07-2019 03:59:00 PM is: The article discusses the recent outage of Mortlake Unit 2, which started the previous morning, and indicates that the plant's operator, Origin Energy, is managing the situation with bids reflective of the current constraints. There is concern over the potential for a longer-term impact on the electricity supply-demand balance, particularly with another unit, Loy Yang A2, already on an extended outage. Additional reports suggest that repairs at Mortlake could take several months, with a return to full capacity possibly by December 20th. The outage poses concern over power supply, especially with the upcoming summer demand. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Mid-July 2019 to December 2019. Its root_cause is: A major explosion causing damage to the turbine. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-07-2019. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Mechanical failure. Its causes are: Explosion at Mortlake Power Station, potential turbine damage, extended repair time, concerns over supply-demand balance with Loy Yang A2 also out.

title: Navigating the grid –  challenges for new solar farm operators
author: Jonathon Dyson
classified_content: The summary of the news "Navigating the grid – challenges for new solar farm operators" posted at 27-06-2019 03:23:00 PM is: The article discusses the challenges facing utility-scale solar farm operators in managing grid constraints and curtailment, based on insights from a recent presentation and the Generator Report Card. Key constraints identified include system normal (voltage management) issues affecting solar assets in New South Wales (NSW) and network outage constraints in Queensland (QLD) during 2019. These constraints impact dispatch outcomes and effective nodal pricing, requiring operators to adapt their generation strategies. The evolving energy landscape necessitates enhanced monitoring and integration of new generator setups, such as solar/battery and wind/battery hybrids, alongside regulatory changes like 5-minute market settlement. The article highlights the shift away from static generator operations to more dynamic, responsive systems in response to ongoing market developments. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2019 and beyond. Its root_cause is: Grid constraints and curtailment in solar generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2019. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users are: Utility-scale solar farm operators. Its cause type is: Grid and network constraints. Its causes are: System normal (voltage management) constraints in NSW and network outage constraints in QLD affecting solar assets.

title: Which was that ‘mystery DUID’ that apparently ‘can’t stand the heat’*?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Which was that ‘mystery DUID’ that apparently ‘can’t stand the heat’?" posted at 26-06-2019 09:06:00 AM is: The Generator Report Card identifies a "mystery DUID" vastly affected in output by high temperatures, later revealed to be the Ararat Wind Farm. This facility's capacity begins to decline beyond 30 degrees Celsius, with significant reductions occurring above 37 degrees. The report underscores the rising reliance of the National Electricity Market (NEM) on weather conditions and the intensified risk this entails. The analysis highlights the necessity for improved visibility of weather impacts on generation technologies and an industry-wide understanding to ensure better preparation for potential temperature extremes. Various causes, including inadequate cooling systems and geographical location, affect such outcomes, with broader implications confirmed in similar wind farms, calling for future investigations. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: High temperature limitations on wind farm outputs. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 26-06-2019. Its affected region is: NEM, specifically inland areas like those hosting the Ararat Wind Farm. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers dependent on wind power within the NEM. Its cause type is: Operational limitation due to environmental factors. Its causes is: High ambient temperature, location in inland regions, European-style temperature protection systems leading to output limitations.

title: Some very preliminary details of the possible causes of the Argentinian Blackout
author: Simon Bartlett
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some very preliminary details of the possible causes of the Argentinian Blackout" posted at 18-06-2019 02:08:00 PM is: The Argentinian blackout on Sunday reportedly resulted from cascading failures in the 500kV grid after a long-term outage of a key transmission line and an unexpected outage of another line. This caused the disconnection of generation stations from the grid, affecting up to 50 million individuals in Argentina and Paraguay for approximately 14 hours. Investigations are ongoing, with energy authorities ruling out cyberattacks and pointing to inadequate infrastructure due to delayed grid upgrades despite sufficient generation and distribution capacity. The situation highlights possible coordination deficiencies in handling line failures and frequency regulation issues in Argentina's grid.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability . Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (lasting up to 14 hours). Its root_cause is: Instability and failure of 500kV grid transmission lines leading to a cascading collapse. Its accident scale is: Large-scale, affecting up to 50 million people and international regions. Its dates are: 16-06-2019 (event date); 18-06-2019 (news date) . Its affected region is: Argentina and parts of Paraguay. Its affected key users are: General population and utility companies. Its cause type is: Technical failure and grid management issues. Its causes are: Long-term transmission line outage, unexpected outage of another line, insufficient grid upgrades, inadequate frequency regulation, and potential lack of coordination in grid management.

title: Extended outage of Loy Yang A unit 2 – reliability and forward price impacts
author: Allan O'Neil
topic: Seasonal Record of Events in the Market
classified_content: The summary of the news "Extended outage of Loy Yang A unit 2 – reliability and forward price impacts" posted at 14-06-2019 04:27:00 PM is: The article discusses the significant implications of an extended outage at Loy Yang A's unit 2, a key generator in Victoria. This outage is expected to influence both reliability and forward electricity prices, particularly extending into early summer. Historical decisions dating back to the 1970s contributed to unique challenges in maintaining the unit, as AGL did not have spare parts for its distinct generator. The Australian Energy Market Operator's MTPASA data shows potential periods of high Loss of Load Probability, especially from November to early December, exacerbated by higher summer demands and the concurrent outages at other generators. While the outage has not significantly affected Victorian spot prices, forward baseload futures contracts for Q3 and Q4 have seen a $5-10/MWh increase, and a large volume of baseload contracts was traded coincidentally with the outage. AGL has reported a potential financial impact of $60-$100 million after tax for FY2019-20, reflecting the cost of replacing the lost low-cost brown coal energy with higher-cost alternatives. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Extending into early December 2019. Its root_cause is: Lack of spare parts and the need for repair of unique generator components. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-06-2019 (referring to analysis data), with outage starting from 18-05-2019. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: AGL and electricity consumers in Victoria. Its cause type is: Mechanical/electrical failure. Its causes are: Generator damage, lack of spares for unique generator model, and necessary repair works extending the outage period.

title: 17-year trend of aggregated coal unit unavailability as from the Generator Report Card
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "17-year trend of aggregated coal unit unavailability as from the Generator Report Card" posted at 14-06-2019 09:11:00 AM is: The article discusses a detailed analysis presented in the Generator Report Card regarding the availability and unavailability trends of coal-fired power units within the National Electricity Market (NEM) over the past 17 years. Highlighted within the report was the observation that the trends in full unavailability remained flat, while partial unavailability displayed a consistent increase, important factors to consider given that coal still contributes 75% of the grid energy within the NEM. This trend analysis is crucial for understanding coal power station dependability and reliability, especially when considering energy transition efforts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures, grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term impact on reliability and operational efficiency. Its root_cause is: Ageing of thermal plants and operational dependability issues. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 14-06-2019 09:11:00 AM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market regions in NSW, QLD, and VIC. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers relying on coal-fired power within the NEM. Its cause type is: Operational reliability issues. Its causes is: Ageing infrastructure leading to increased partial unavailability.

title: Brief comment on the extended outage at Loy Yang A unit 2
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief comment on the extended outage at Loy Yang A unit 2" posted at 10-06-2019 08:37:00 AM is: The outage at Loy Yang A unit 2, which occurred on May 18, 2019, has raised significant concerns due to an ASX notice from AGL indicating that the outage could extend up to seven months, potentially impacting the summer of 2019-20. This has sparked widespread media and social media discussions, highlighting issues of grid stability during extreme temperatures and reduced dispatchable plant availability. The risk has been further escalated by misconceptions and a lack of energy literacy. Considering such outages during peak demand can stress the grid significantly; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Potentially extending into the summer of 2019-20. Its root_cause is: Turbine/generator failure at Loy Yang A unit 2. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18th May 2019 (initial outage), 7th June 2019 (AGL notice). Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly in Victoria and possibly South Australia. Its cause type is: Mechanical failure. Its causes are: Reliance on aging assets, misinterpretations of asset dependability, and the general escalation of risk in energy management.

title: Upcoming Q&A sessions on the Generator Report Card
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Upcoming Q&A sessions on the Generator Report Card" posted at 05-06-2019 10:26:00 AM is: Global-Roam and Greenview Strategic Consulting have released the Generator Report Card after a seven-month collaborative effort, and are organizing informal Q&A sessions in various capital cities to engage with clients and gather feedback. These sessions are designed for clients who have already received the report card and for those curious about its contents. The Q&A sessions occurred in Melbourne, Adelaide, Brisbane, and a future session was scheduled for Sydney on July 29. Attendees are limited to a maximum of two per organization to facilitate in-depth conversations. Stakeholders are encouraged to register for the Sydney session while spots last.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy . Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Initiating stakeholder engagement for feedback on the Generator Report Card. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-06-2019 (main), 11-06-2019 (Melbourne session), 27-06-2019 (Adelaide session), 09-07-2019 (Brisbane session), 29-07-2019 (Sydney session). Its affected region is: Capital cities in Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market stakeholders and clients of Global-Roam and Greenview Strategic Consulting. Its cause type is: Organisational initiative. Its causes are: Release and promotion of the Generator Report Card, Stakeholder engagement for feedback.

title: Five different aspects of assessing the “dependability” of coal-fired power (in the Generator Report Card)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Five different aspects of assessing the “dependability” of coal-fired power (in the Generator Report Card)" posted at 03-06-2019 08:53:00 AM is: The article discusses a section of the Generator Report Card, focusing on the assessment of the "dependability" of coal-fired power in the National Electricity Market (NEM). It outlines how the dependability has changed over time, based on two decades of historical performance data. The analysis aims to provide insights into coal-fired power's reliability, which is crucial for understanding its role in emissions reduction strategies.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Historical performance of coal-fired power. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-06-2019 08:53:00 AM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Energy producers, policymakers, and consumers concerned with emissions reduction. Its cause type is: Analytical review. Its causes are: Changes in coal-fired power dependability over 20 years.

title: Some early comments made about the Generator Report Card
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some early comments made about the Generator Report Card" posted at 03-06-2019 08:51:00 AM is: The Generator Report Card was launched on 31st May, offering detailed analysis of the National Electricity Market (NEM) with a 530-page report provided to clients. Media outlets and social media have reacted, highlighting its independence and potential benefits to new market entrants and industry analysts, particularly in identifying opportunities and risks. The report is considered valuable by clients for its broad analysis, covering trends such as the decline in coal trips and the anti-correlation between wind and solar. Feedback emphasizes its depth, with discussions surrounding its comprehensive data review significant for stakeholders. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Compilation and release of the Generator Report Card. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 03-06-2019. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: New market entrants and industry analysts. Its cause type is: Report publication. Its causes is: Development and release of a detailed report on the NEM aiming to provide data-driven insights for energy market participants.

title: Generator Report Card released (on Friday 31st May 2019)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Generator Report Card released (on Friday 31st May 2019)" posted at 31-05-2019 07:10:00 AM is: The Generator Report Card was released by Global-Roam Pty Ltd and Greenview Strategic Consulting on May 31, 2019, after a collaborative effort spanning seven months. This comprehensive 530-page report provides an in-depth analysis of various aspects of generator performance, particularly in relation to energy transition. Clients who pre-ordered had early electronic access and a physical copy is available for an additional fee; feedback is solicited. Informal Q&A sessions are planned in Melbourne, Brisbane, and Sydney in June and July. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Release of a comprehensive report on generator performance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-05-2019. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Clients and stakeholders in energy markets. Its cause type is: Data analysis/report release. Its causes are: Collaboration between Global-Roam Pty Ltd and Greenview Strategic Consulting, energy transition analysis.

title: A page for each DUID in (Part 3 of) the Generator Report Card
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A page for each DUID in (Part 3 of) the Generator Report Card" posted at 26-04-2019 12:38:00 PM is: The Generator Report Card is nearing completion, with Part 3 focusing on the analysis of DUIDs over a 10-year period. Drafts are being reviewed before the release following the Federal Election, using vast data sets to provide a detailed operational review. Feedback has led to explanations of the report's structure, and pre-orders are still being accepted with early-bird pricing available. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-04-2019 12:38:00 PM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes are: Unknown.

title: Degradation of performance at high temperatures
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Degradation of performance at high temperatures" posted at 18-04-2019 03:52:00 PM is: The article discusses the performance degradation of fossil-fired thermal plants in high-temperature conditions in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). As extreme temperatures become more common, the impact on electricity production efficiency is increasingly significant, prompting ongoing analysis of operational Demand Unit Identifiers (DUIDs) across all fuel types. The findings will be part of a comprehensive Generator Report Card due after the upcoming federal election on May 18th. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, extending into future decades with continued extreme temperature occurrences. Its root_cause is: High temperatures leading to reduced efficiency in fossil-fired thermal power plants. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-04-2019. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: Extreme temperatures impacting thermal plant efficiency.

title: Despite growing diversity of Large Solar farms (NEM-wide), large weather events still have major dampening effects
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Despite growing diversity of Large Solar farms (NEM-wide), large weather events still have major dampening effects" posted at 01-04-2019 03:08:00 PM is: An extensive analysis of 20 years of generation performance data revealed that despite the growth in large solar capacity across the National Electricity Market (NEM), significant reductions in solar output are still observed during major weather events. Recent occurrences include the aftermath of Tropical Cyclone Trevor in late March 2019, which reduced aggregate solar output by almost half, and the north Queensland flooding in late January to early February 2019, which significantly affected solar production in the area. These findings highlight the vulnerability of solar power generation to extreme weather conditions. The completion and release of a detailed Generator Report Card have been delayed due to political climate considerations surrounding the upcoming Federal Election.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Late March 2019 for Cyclone Trevor and late January to early February 2019 for the Queensland deluge. Root_cause is: Extreme weather events such as cyclones and floods. Accident scale is: NEM-wide for solar output reduction. Dates are: Late March 2019, late January to early February 2019, 27th June 2018, 8th and 9th June 2018. Affected region is: North Queensland and potentially NEM-wide impact. Affected key users are: Solar energy producers and consumers relying on solar energy. Cause type is: Natural/weather-related. Causes are: Tropical Cyclone Trevor in March 2019 and north Queensland flooding in early 2019.

title: About claims that AGL was responsible for the uplift in prices after Hazelwood closed
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "About claims that AGL was responsible for the uplift in prices after Hazelwood closed" posted at 27-03-2019 09:30:00 PM is: The article discusses the ongoing impact and controversy surrounding the closure of the Hazelwood Power Station on Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) two years post-closure. The closure, announced in November 2016 and completed by March 2017, significantly affected market dynamics, resulting in rising electricity prices attributed to reduced supply. It sparked accusations against major players like AGL of exercising market power to inflate prices, triggering investigations by the Australian Energy Regulator (AER) and the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC). The debate intensified with recent reports questioning whether current market structures and regulations facilitate fair competition. A call for increased competition, possibly through rule changes and structural reforms, is highlighted as a potential solution to stabilize prices. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: March 2017 to March 2019 and beyond. Its root_cause is: Closure of Hazelwood Power Station reducing supply capacity in the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: March 2017 (closure date) to the article date March 2019. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (spanning eastern and southeastern Australia). Its affected key users are: All electricity consumers within the NEM, particularly large energy users. Its cause type is: Market structural changes. Its causes are: Closure of Hazelwood, subsequent market power by generators, regulatory and competition inquiries, limited supply capacity, and price dynamics.

title: Peak Wind Output in the NEM has climbed above 4000MW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Peak Wind Output in the NEM has climbed above 4000MW" posted at 25-03-2019 08:19:00 AM is: The news article discusses a recent observation from the NEMwatch dashboard showing that wind generation in the National Electricity Market (NEM) peaked at around 3,900MW at 04:45 NEM time on the morning of 25th March 2019. This figure is just below the all-time high of approximately 4,050MW recorded on 12th February 2019. The article notes that despite consistent wind outputs, the mainland electricity prices were high, around $100/MWh. This suggests complexity in price-setting mechanisms that aren't explained solely by wind generation levels. No specific accident or disruption events were noted, and the article focuses on statistical analysis rather than immediate crises.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown, likely ongoing observation. Its root_cause is: The statistical observation of wind generation output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-03-2019 and reference to 12-02-2019. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (Australia). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Statistical observation. Its causes are: The peak wind output reaching close to 4,000MW and an observation on price despite high wind generation.

title: Price Setting Concepts – an Explainer
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price Setting Concepts – an Explainer" posted at 22-03-2019 12:21:00 PM is: The article provides an in-depth examination of the principles governing price setting and dispatch in the National Electricity Market (NEM), highlighting key concepts such as least-cost dispatch, marginal cost pricing, and the treatment of transmission losses. It explains the foundational process of bid stacking and its application in real-world scenarios, discussing factors like intra-regional and inter-regional loss adjustments, and the complexities of dynamic pricing when transmission constraints are present. The guide also elucidates how a single generating unit in one region can set prices in multiple regions due to interconnectedness, and it outlines principles for understanding spot prices and price-setting in interconnected electricity markets.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term structural impacts. Its root cause is: Understanding of electricity market dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-03-2019. Its affected region is: Australia (NEM regions). Its affected key users are: Energy market analysts and participants. Its cause type is: Structural and educational insight. Its causes are: NEM price setting principles, least-cost dispatch, treatment of transmission losses, interconnector constraints, market operation insights.

title: A twenty year review of Marginal Loss Factors
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A twenty year review of Marginal Loss Factors" posted at 11-03-2019 08:16:00 AM is: The article discusses the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) recent draft release of Marginal Loss Factors (MLFs) and their significant impact on the financial outcomes for market participants in the National Electricity Market (NEM). It explains how MLFs have historically varied based on generator locations and grid connections, affecting the cost and competitiveness of electricity generation. There is a considerable social media response regarding these changes, with concerns around fairness and unexpected outcomes, particularly for wind and solar plants. The ongoing changes have been influenced by a range of factors including increased generation in electrically weak and remote areas, leading to declining MLFs. Adani Renewables has proposed rule changes to address these issues. The report emphasizes the importance of understanding MLFs within the context of the power system's physics and market dynamics.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing and potentially long-term as updates are released annually and market dynamics evolve. Root_cause is: Changes in the generation mix and grid connections affecting MLFs and market financials. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: Mid-2018 (previous changes noted) and proposed rule changes in December 2018 and February 2019. Affected region is: National Electricity Market, Australia. Affected key users are: Electricity generators, particularly those in remote or congested areas of the grid. Cause type is: Structural and regulatory factors in the electricity market. Causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Changes in generation locations, grid congestion, regulatory frameworks, market reactions to MLF adjustments.

title: A (Much) Deeper Dive into Friday March 1 2019
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "A (Much) Deeper Dive into Friday March 1 2019" posted at 08-03-2019 02:00:00 PM is: The article provides a comprehensive analysis of the events in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on March 1, 2019, focusing on Victoria and South Australia. Hot weather resulted in significant price volatility, exacerbated by complex bidding behaviors, demand forecasts, and constraints on interconnectors. Despite high demands, overall generation supply was adequate, with significant input from brown coal, gas, and hydro sources. The article explores the issues with wind generation predictability, demand response activities such as those at the Portland Aluminium Smelter, and interregional power transfer limitations due to system constraints. The analysis concludes that high prices were driven by offers only at high levels rather than a physical supply shortfall.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and transient, affecting real-time pricing on March 1, 2019. Its root cause is: High temperature leading to increased electricity demand, coupled with complex market dynamics and system constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: March 1, 2019. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential and industrial electricity consumers, particularly those with high demand during the peak period. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand increase, market bidding behavior, and operational constraints on power flow. Its causes are: High temperatures increasing demand, significant market bidding and rebidding creating price spikes, interconnector limitations affecting power flow, and reduced effectiveness of rooftop solar during critical periods.

title: The Network Investment Paradox
author: Drew Donnelly
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Network Investment Paradox" posted at 07-03-2019 02:23:00 PM is: The article discusses the paradox of network investment, highlighting cases in Buenos Aires and Australia. In Buenos Aires, under-investment in distribution infrastructure due to frozen electricity prices and inflation has led to power outages amid increasing demand. Meanwhile, Australia has faced issues of over-investment in its network, causing inflated retail prices due to inefficient regulatory settings. The article examines the consequences of both under and over-investment, emphasizing the need for balanced regulatory policies. The impact includes sharp retail price increases in Buenos Aires and a leveling off of price rises in Australia due to various regulatory measures. The article also discusses the emerging challenges posed by embedded networks and distributed energy resources in Australia. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with noted impacts around 2017 to 2019. Its root_cause is: Regulatory imbalance leading to under-investment in Buenos Aires and over-investment in Australia. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-03-2019 (main date), with events starting from the early 1990s in Buenos Aires and ongoing issues in Australia. Its affected region is: Buenos Aires, Argentina, and Australia. Its affected key users are: Consumers in Buenos Aires facing blackouts and increased prices, and Australian consumers impacted by higher retail prices. Its cause type is: Economic and regulatory. Its causes are: Economic crisis in Buenos Aires, frozen electricity prices, inflation, regulatory settings in Australia enabling over-investment, and emergent trends in embedded networks and DER affecting future investments.

title: The death* of “baseload” and rise of “cycling”
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The death* of “baseload” and rise of “cycling”" posted at 07-03-2019 06:20:00 AM is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia is experiencing a transition from a "baseload" model to a "cycling" system, as evidenced by an increase in unit cycles due to the rise of solar and other renewable sources. This shift impacts pricing patterns, creates uncertainty for large energy users traditionally reliant on consistent baseload supply, and necessitates greater flexibility and responsiveness in both demand and supply sides of the market. Additionally, the complexity of orchestration will escalate, requiring more advanced metrics and strategies to manage grid stability, and likely altering the landscape of hedging in the energy market; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term, evolving over decades. Its root cause is: Transition from traditional baseload models to more dynamic, renewable-centric generation systems. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-03-2019 06:20:00 AM. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Large energy consumers and market participants within the NEM. Its cause type is: Systemic transition. Its causes are: Increased unit cycling, renewable energy integration, changes in demand response requirements, and evolving grid management complexities.

title: Some thoughts about Demand Response, in parallel with AEMC deliberations
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some thoughts about Demand Response, in parallel with AEMC deliberations" posted at 05-03-2019 10:30:00 AM is: The article discusses considerations surrounding Demand Response within the National Electricity Market (NEM) in light of the Australian Energy Market Commission's (AEMC) deliberation on rule change proposals. It highlights the historical context, current state, and future prospects of Demand Response, emphasizing the need for increased flexibility in energy consumption due to rising intermittent renewables. Despite a decade of industry involvement, the article raises concerns that current rule change proposals might not effectively enable necessary market demands. It suggests exploring more sustainable approaches, such as a genuine two-sided market and increasing transparency. The author expresses keen interest in intentional participation if meaningful directions are pursued, although underlines significant time constraints and inherent risks tied to proposed market changes.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: Unknown. Its root_cause is: The ongoing deliberations and proposed rule changes by the AEMC for Demand Response mechanisms in the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 05-03-2019. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy users involved in Demand Response programs. Its cause type is: Institutional deliberation and decision-making on energy policy. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Deliberations by the AEMC, proposals for rule changes in Demand Response, historical and ongoing energy market transitions, and identified needs for system flexibility.

title: Villain #6c – Demand Response is not a magic wand
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Villain #6c – Demand Response is not a magic wand" posted at 05-03-2019 10:28:00 AM is: The article discusses the common misconceptions about Demand Response as a solution within the energy transition sector, highlighting that such approaches are often seen as quick fixes or "magic wands" without considering the complexity of supply and demand challenges. The author expresses skepticism about viewing Demand Response, along with baseload and battery storage, as catch-all solutions for the energy grid's issues. Reminding readers that the primary goal for energy users is to utilize electricity to perform useful work, not just to maintain grid stability, the article critiques the tendency to ignore the broader objectives of energy use. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, as it involves ongoing policy and market perspectives. Its root_cause is: Misplaced assumptions about Demand Response's effectiveness as a solution. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-03-2019 10:28:00 AM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Energy policy and market stakeholders. Its cause type is: Misunderstanding/misperception. Its causes are: Misconceptions about Demand Response, reliance on simplistic solutions in energy policy discourse.

title: Villain #5d – obsessing about Negawatts, when the real objective is (broader) Demand Response
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Villain #5d – obsessing about Negawatts, when the real objective is (broader) Demand Response" posted at 05-03-2019 10:25:00 AM is: The article discusses concerns regarding the focus on "Negawatts" as a supply-side option, potentially overshadowing the broader objective of Demand Response. The Australian Energy Market Commission's (AEMC) discussions on increasing demand response face the risk of a narrow focus on Negawatts, neglecting other measures that could optimally balance supply and demand in the energy market. Demand Response encompasses a variety of strategies to enhance electricity consumption flexibility, which is essential for a smoother energy transition. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with implications as discussions and policy developments continue. Its root_cause is: A narrow focus on Negawatts in energy policy deliberations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-03-2019. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy policymakers and stakeholders in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its cause type is: Policy focus. Its causes are: Obsession with Negawatts as a supply-side option, overshadowing broader demand response strategies.

title: A brief look back at reactions to negative prices in the 14:30 trading period on Friday 1st March 2019
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A brief look back at reactions to negative prices in the 14:30 trading period on Friday 1st March 2019" posted at 02-03-2019 03:05:00 PM is: This article discusses the reactions to the negative dispatch prices during the 14:30 trading period on Friday, March 1, 2019, in the National Electricity Market (NEM). It examines the operations of five power stations – Karadoc Solar Farm, Gannawarra Solar Farm, Loy Yang A, Loy Yang B, and Yallourn – which experienced reduced output because their bid volumes exceeded the market price. This strategic reduction helped mitigate potential revenue losses in an unusual pricing scenario often characterized by market price volatility. While some interpretations initially blamed system anomalies or failures, the analysis concludes these outages were consistent with normal market operations. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate impact during the trading period on March 1, 2019. Its root cause is: Normal market operations due to strategic bidding. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-03-2019. Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC), South Australia (SA), and Tasmania (TAS). Its affected key users are: Power generators and energy market traders. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes are: Negative dispatch prices, strategic bidding by power stations, reduction in output according to bid profiles.

title: Price volatility in VIC and SA on Friday 1st March
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility in VIC and SA on Friday 1st March" posted at 01-03-2019 04:33:00 PM is: The first day of Autumn in Victoria and South Australia saw significant price volatility in the National Electricity Market (NEM), with price spikes throughout the afternoon driven by trader behavior and the complexity of price determination. This volatility was exacerbated by low wind production and a simultaneous trip at Gannawarra and Karadoc solar farms, while all brown coal units in Victoria were operational and most gas/liquid peakers were running. The event is ongoing, with some patterns potentially indicating deeper underlying causes. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term and ongoing as of the news posting. Its root_cause is: Price forecast errors and operational anomalies in energy production. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-03-2019. Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC) and South Australia (SA). Its affected key users are: Residential demand response customers and those at the Commercial & Industrial (C&I) level. Its cause type is: Operational and forecast inaccuracies. Its causes are: Trader behavior, low wind production, solar farm trips, and high operational demand on gas/liquid peakers.

title: Lessons Learnt from Amber’s first summer of Spot Exposure in the NEM
author: Chris Thompson
classified_content: The summary of the news "Lessons Learnt from Amber’s first summer of Spot Exposure in the NEM" posted at 01-03-2019 09:40:00 AM is: Amber Electric's first summer in the National Electricity Market highlighted the potential and challenges of passing wholesale electricity prices to residential consumers. Customers in NSW and SA experienced savings by shifting consumption to times with lower wholesale rates. However, a significant price spike on January 24th in SA tested this model's viability, contrasting potential savings with extreme volatility risks. The event underscored the need for both manual and automated demand response to engage and benefit consumers effectively. The unexpected effectiveness of rooftop solar as a price hedge was also recognized, several lessons from this summer demonstrated that for the model to reach its full potential, consumer engagement and automation are vital; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Residential satisfaction. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Summer 2018/2019. The root_cause is: Retail model challenges under volatile wholesale pricing. Its accident scale is: No physical accident; impact relates to price volatility. The dates are: January 24th, 2019, for the specific price spike event. Its affected region is: New South Wales and South Australia. The affected key users are: Residential electricity consumers. The cause type is: Market pricing model and demand response inefficiencies. The causes are: Lack of consumer engagement, reliance on manual demand response, and challenges in managing extreme price volatility.

title: A (preliminary) Intermediate Guide to How Prices are Set in the NEM
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A (preliminary) Intermediate Guide to How Prices are Set in the NEM" posted at 28-02-2019 02:07:00 PM is: The article delves into the complex process of electricity pricing in the National Electricity Market (NEM), emphasizing that prices are set by the marginal unit, typically gas and hydro generators, reflecting the intricate supply-demand dynamics. This complexity is explored through the "Price Setter" files by AEMO, which provide insights into how prices are determined for each dispatch interval across different regions. The article categorizes price setting from very simple (single DUID influence) to very complex (multi-factor, including FCAS contributions) and highlights how most pricing is more complex than what's perceived by general observers. This preliminary guide aims to elucidate the multifaceted realities of price setting beyond simple theoretical models. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with insights impacting analysis for future market operations and policy decisions. Its root_cause is: Complex market dynamics and the need to understand detailed pricing mechanisms. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-02-2019, with references to historical data from 01-01-2018 to 05-02-2019. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market regions in Australia. Its affected key users are: Wholesale electricity market participants, analysts, and policymakers. Its cause type is: Analytical exploration of pricing mechanisms. Its causes are: Complexity in price setting mechanisms, marginal unit pricing, and multi-dimensional factors like FCAS involvement.

title: It’s heating up, in Victoria and South Australia for Friday 1st March
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "It’s heating up, in Victoria and South Australia for Friday 1st March" posted at 27-02-2019 05:33:00 PM is: The article discusses a forecasted heatwave for South Australia and Victoria on Friday, 1st March, marking the end of the summer season. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a Market Notice regarding a forecast Lack Of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition expected for that day, a rare occurrence likely influenced by factors such as the closure of Hazelwood and changes in PASA processes. In addition, there was a notice about "Generator Recall Information," which is not a common event. Demand forecasts for both regions are high, though not record-breaking. The anticipated decrease in intermittent generation output due to poorer wind conditions amidst the heat has prompted the recall of scheduled plants to ensure stability in various supply/demand scenarios. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short term, specifically around 1st March 2019. Its root_cause is: Forecasted hot weather conditions leading to increased electricity demand and decreased wind generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 1st March 2019. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Victoria and South Australia. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: Forecasted hot weather, decreased wind generation, high electricity demand, and generator recalls.

title: Factors contributing to an unforeseen price spike in NSW at 16:15 on Thursday 31st January 2019
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Factors contributing to an unforeseen price spike in NSW at 16:15 on Thursday 31st January 2019" posted at 21-02-2019 08:45:00 AM is: The article investigates a sudden electricity price surge in New South Wales at 16:15 on January 31, 2019, which affected energy users with spot market exposure. Contributing factors include an unexpected rise in demand, a drop in intermittent supply, reduced imports from Victoria due to binding constraints, and coal supply issues affecting Mt Piper Unit 2. These conditions highlight the complexities amid market transitions and challenges in demand response practices; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Fuel supply issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term. Its root_cause is: Unforeseen demand increase and supply constraints. Its accident scale is: Localized. Its dates is: 31-01-2019. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Energy users with spot market exposure. Its cause type is: Market and operational changes. Its causes is: Unexpected demand increase, intermittent supply drop, reduction in imports from Victoria, coal supply constraints at Mt Piper.

title: New all-time record for electricity demand in Queensland on Wednesday 13th February 2019
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New all-time record for electricity demand in Queensland on Wednesday 13th February 2019" posted at 13-02-2019 09:53:00 PM is: Queensland set a new record for electricity demand on February 13th, 2019, reaching 10,052MW as residents increased air-conditioning usage in response to high temperatures, coinciding with a decrease in solar PV injections in the late afternoon. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) had forecasted this peak, which highlights the complexities of electricity demand metrics and the role of solar power. The event demonstrates the challenges AEMO faces in balancing supply and demand, particularly as the transition towards a higher solar contribution progresses. Discussions also centered around the de-rating of solar systems, attributed to network voltage issues, high temperatures, and potential degradation of solar panels. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: High temperatures increasing demand and reducing solar output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-02-2019. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial users. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand surge. Its causes are: High temperatures, increased air-conditioning usage, reduction in solar energy contribution.

title: Very preliminary review of Quantity and Quality of Rebids suggests some interesting details…
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Very preliminary review of Quantity and Quality of Rebids suggests some interesting details…" posted at 13-02-2019 02:39:00 PM is: The article examines the preliminary analysis of bidding and rebidding patterns in the National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly noting the rising trend of rebids for wind farms and the high percentage of bids classified as "Not Well Formed" according to AER guidelines. The study highlights challenges faced by wind farm operators due to constraint equations and negative pricing events, primarily in South Australia, leading to frequent rebidding. Additionally, the analysis identifies a significant proportion of bids not adhering strictly to AER’s guidelines, though they often meet the guidelines' general intent. The findings are part of the upcoming Generator Report Card 2018, and further detailed analysis is planned.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since mid-2010, with increased activity from 2017 into 2018. Its root_cause is: Wind farm operators responding to market conditions and regulatory constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 13-02-2019. Its affected region is: Primarily South Australia. Its affected key users is: Wind farm operators in the NEM. Its cause type is: Market and regulatory-driven. Its causes is: 1) Constraints imposed by the Semi-Dispatch Cap; 2) Negative pricing events; 3) AER guideline interpretations for rebidding.

title: Looks set for a peak demand in QLD on Wednesday up near the all-time maximum
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Looks set for a peak demand in QLD on Wednesday up near the all-time maximum" posted at 11-02-2019 09:23:00 AM is: The article discusses the forecast of potentially all-time high electricity demand in Queensland on an upcoming Wednesday, near 9,835 MW during peak time, driven by summer conditions and high underlying demand. The growth of both large-scale and small-scale solar PV in Queensland plays a significant role in netting off demand figures, but challenges remain in accurately measuring small-scale PV contributions. Updated AEMO forecasts are constantly evolving, with interest from stakeholders in observing how current demand trends compare to historical peaks.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specific to Wednesday's forecasted peak. Its root_cause is: High summer temperatures leading to increased electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 11-02-2019. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related demand surge. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: High underlying demand due to summer heat, contribution impact of solar PV systems.

title: AGL Investor Presentation reports on Portfolio Availability–we take a look for key Stations
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AGL Investor Presentation reports on Portfolio Availability–we take a look for key Stations" posted at 08-02-2019 12:56:00 PM is: The AGL Energy 2019 Half-Year Results Presentation to investors revealed detailed availability data for its thermal plant portfolio, specifically focusing on coal units at Loy Yang A, Bayswater, and Liddell. The analysis highlights downward trends in availability over recent years, with cyclical patterns due to maintenance and outages also noted. This preliminary data is part of a larger ongoing analysis for the Generator Report Card 2018. The Liddell Power Station, in particular, shows a significant and consistent decline in availability, correlating with its potential closure discussions.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term, as the ongoing decline in availability could affect future supply and market dynamics. Its root_cause is: Plant availability and maintenance issues. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-02-2019 (as referenced data). Its affected region is: New South Wales (primarily where the key stations are located). Its affected key users are: Energy markets and potentially AGL investors. Its cause type is: Operational inefficiencies and maintenance cycles. Its causes are: Declining availability trends at key power stations.

title: 2018 – the year fossil fuels began an inexorable decline
author: Tristan Edis
classified_content: The summary of the news "2018 – the year fossil fuels began an inexorable decline" posted at 07-02-2019 12:33:00 PM is: In 2018, Australia's electricity generation saw a significant shift as renewable energy reached a market share of 21.3%, surpassing the 20% threshold for the first time since the 1970s, and marking a notable decline in reliance on fossil fuels. The east coast National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced declines in coal and gas generation, with gas dropping significantly by 26%.
